story:
Duncan lay splayed—legs on the bed, arms over the nightstand—warming in the late afternoon sun when Lynette found him and gasped. Nicole shadowed her, eager for a show. Lynette gently untangled Duncan’s strings and rods, handling him the way you would a fragile creature and not a marionette. Nicole cackled, “Gonna cry over your creepy doll?” When Lynette warned, “Don’t touch Duncan,” her voice cracked—fuel for Nicole’s taunts. “Poor baby. Pobrecita.” Lynette’s “I hate you!” summoned their mother, Maria, who stomped in like a disgruntled rhino, screeching threats about telling their father—the daily script that impressed nobody.

Nicole darted to the living room phone to broadcast the scandal to Vanessa: Lynette still played with dolls. Maria fled to her room to lock the door and wallow. Lynette slipped Duncan into a pillowcase, added Buck, a small red plastic pony, and walked right past Nicole and out the front door. She wasn’t allowed out alone. She wasn’t sure if she was escaping or trying to make them miss her. Either way, the only place she knew how to reach in Los Búhos—a one-mile town with one library, two schools, three bars, six churches—was the library.

Terrified and brave, she made it to the glass doors, performed a confident strut past the front-desk librarian (librarians know everything; surely they’d spot a runaway), and descended to the children’s section. Hidden between long rows of books, she opened the pillowcase, whispered, “We made it,” then browsed like a regular patron. A title hooked her: The Red Pony by John Steinbeck. A ten-year-old boy given a pony by his rancher father—exactly the life she wanted to believe in. She sat cross-legged, tucking the pillowcase against her calves, and read. Turns out, a pony is not a magic wand, and not all rancher dads are rancher-dad perfect.

What felt like twenty minutes was two hours. Then she heard it: her father’s voice, asking the children’s librarian if anyone had seen her. He’d left work—he never left work. Lynette peeked and felt the jolt. That’s him. Busted. She huddled deeper in the aisle as Cal Smith passed the end of her row without looking her way, heading for the stairs. Frozen, then flooded by the urge to run, she left the book on the floor and sprinted past the children’s desk. The librarian called, “Wait!” No chance. Lynette slipped out the basement exit, zigzagged down Main Street, cut past the pharmacy, and hid in the baseball dugout. She wanted a destination; there wasn’t one for a ten-year-old with no money on a school night. She promised Duncan and Buck they’d ride a train out of town someday, then turned home.

She stashed the pillowcase in the bushes, slipped inside hoping to go unnoticed. They noticed. Maria’s concern flashed, then curdled: “You’re selfish. You don’t give a damn about anyone but yourself.” She stomped away and locked her door. Cal’s voice was low, flat, supplying the lie for her: “Around the block, eh?” Lynette held to the lie. He surprised her: “You’re not the only one who gets sick of everyone’s stuff around here.” Then, almost casual: “The Red Pony—any good?” She admitted it was. “Sarah from the library called,” he said, laughing. He asked her to give Mom a break. “Probably not, but I’ll try.” “Just try,” he said, and left for work.

Lynette retrieved Duncan and Buck and whispered, “We did it,” without knowing what “it” was. Nicole drifted in with her soft, baiting voice. “Where’d you really go?” “I’m not talking to you.” Nicole scoffed and left. Lynette lay back, stunned that her father hadn’t exploded, sad that nothing else had changed. She set Duncan on the windowsill, legs swinging, Buck at his side, and let the daylight hold them a little longer.
